summary_30s: High energy bills usually mean the system is running too long due to airflow restriction, poor heat transfer, or refrigerant imbalance. Runtime inflation shows up before comfort fully collapses—static pressure and sensible split tell the story.
decision_tree:
If bills are rising → check runtime versus design hours.
If system runs constantly → airflow or load issue is primary.
If airflow is weak → airflow restriction drives watt draw up.
If airflow is normal → check refrigerant pressures in PSI next.
If suction PSI is low → low charge equals a leak because refrigerant is not consumed.
If amps are high at the same setpoint → inefficiency or overwork at a component is likely.
If fan running but equipment never reaches setpoint → system working is not proven by blower motion.
top_causes: Airflow restriction increases static pressure and reduces heat transfer. Refrigerant is not consumed, so low charge equals a leak. Dirty coils, duct restrictions, and improper sizing all increase runtime and energy consumption.
how_system_works: An AC system should cycle off once the set temperature is reached. Airflow carries heat to the evaporator coil for heat transfer. If airflow restriction or refrigerant imbalance exists, the system runs longer and harder. Ignoring this forces the compressor outside its design limits. A fan running does not mean the system working.
diagnostic_steps:
Measure temperature split in °F across the coil; if below 16°F → inefficient cooling or airflow fault.
Measure suction pressure in PSI; if low → leak confirmed because refrigerant is not consumed.
Measure system amps and line voltage (VAC); if amps exceed rating while setpoint drags → overload fault is present.
If airflow, thermostat, and power are confirmed and the system still is not cooling, the fault is no longer superficial. continuing to run the system is what turns a manageable repair into a major failure.
Stop. Professional diagnosis is not optional before declaring any single component bad.
cost_matrix: Tune-up: $150–$400 | Coil cleaning: $300–$800 | Leak repair: $500–$1500 | Replacement: $5000–$12000
replace_vs_repair: If repair is $800 vs replacement $8000, repair may make sense under 10 years. If inefficiency persists or the system is over 12 years old, it is better to replace. Chronic inefficiency is not worth repairing repeatedly.
stop_diy: Stop. Professional diagnosis is not optional. This is how minor complaints turn into compressor failures.
prevention_tips: Maintain airflow, clean coils, monitor runtime, and keep static pressure within manufacturer windows.
tools_needed: Thermometer, amp clamp, manifold gauges, multimeter
bench_test_notes: Measure amps and line voltage (VAC). If amps exceed rated load → indicates system inefficiency and potential component failure. Measure PSI. Low PSI confirms refrigerant fault. Measure airflow. Low airflow indicates duct restriction fault.
